metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docs/coverage.md (or CI docs) update — covering the xdist + parallel=true coverage-combine requirement — to at least one child's scope and acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: Parent acceptance criterion AC-3 — 'docs/coverage.md (and any CI docs) updated to note the xdist + parallel=true coverage-combine requirement' — is not covered by any child in the sibling roster.
findings.location: Parent AC-3; absent from children 8d36, 9ad9, bad4
findings.suggested_fix: Extend child 8d36's scope and AC to include: 'Update docs/coverage.md to document that parallel=true is required in [tool.coverage.run] when running under pytest-xdist, and that .coverage.* files are combined automatically; note the fail_under=70 threshold still applies to the combined report.'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit landing order (8d36 before 9ad9, or co-land as a single Gerrit topic) and note the dependency in child 9ad9's description.
findings.finding: Children 8d36 and 9ad9 both rewrite the same pytest command lines in gerrit-verify.yaml and test.yml with no declared ordering or co-landing requirement, creating an implicit ordering violation.
findings.location: child 8d36 (gerrit-verify.yaml / test.yml default-suite command); child 9ad9 (same files, same command)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 9ad9's description: 'Depends on 8d36 landing first (or co-landed in the same topic); --durations=25 must be appended to the already-restructured xdist command, not the legacy serial command.' Alternatively, combine both changes into one Gerrit change set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note in child 9ad9 that the optional --store-durations step requires pytest-split (added by child 8d36) and must not be activated until 8d36 has landed.
findings.finding: Child 9ad9's optional --store-durations step implicitly depends on pytest-split being installed, which is only added to [dev] extras by child 8d36 — but child 9ad9 does not name 8d36 as a prerequisite, creating an unacknowledged dependency gap.
findings.location: child 9ad9 (What section, optional --store-durations step); child 8d36 (pyproject.toml [dev] deps)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 9ad9's description under the optional item: 'Prerequisite: pytest-split must be present in [dev] (added by child 8d36). Do not activate this step until 8d36 is merged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact pytest-xdist invocation in the normative scope/AC (e.g., `-n auto --dist worksteal`) so the executor has no discretion on mechanism.
findings.finding: The success criterion 'runs under pytest-xdist pinned to the runner's real core count' does not specify HOW the core count is determined (e.g., `-n auto`, `-n $(nproc)`, a hardcoded `-n 4`, or a CI env var), nor does it specify which `--dist` mode to use; the context shows `worksteal` was tested but this never appears in a normative section.
findings.location: Success Criteria — first bullet; Context paragraph mentioning `-n4 --dist worksteal`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pinned to the runner's real core count' with 'invoked as `-n auto --dist worksteal`' (matching the validated experiment) and note that `-n auto` resolves to the runner's reported CPU count at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen the pip-cache criterion to a specific observable artifact and threshold, e.g., 'the `setup-python` step reports a cache hit on the second run and install completes in ≤12 s on the GitHub-hosted runner (down from ~19 s cold)'.
findings.finding: The success criterion 'pip cache hit reduces per-job install time on repeat runs' uses a vague verb ('reduces') with no threshold, no specific observable artifact, and requires two sequential CI runs (cold then warm) to verify — making it difficult to evaluate deterministically in a single session.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pip cache hit reduces per-job install time on repeat runs' with 'the `setup-python` action reports `Cache hit: true` on the second run of any matrix leg, and the install step completes in ≤12 s (baseline: ~19 s cold, per ticket 7654 measurements)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align terminology: replace one of 'default suite' (Context) or 'default tier' (Scope) with a single canonical term, or add a parenthetical clarifying they are the same `-m "not integration and not external"` selection.
findings.finding: The Scope section describes 'the default tier' as the parallelization target, while the Context section uses 'default suite' for the same test set. These are the same concept but named differently across sections, creating potential implementer confusion about whether they refer to the same test selection.
findings.location: Context ('default suite, 5,701 tests') vs Scope IN ('parallelization of the default tier')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, change 'default tier' to 'default suite (the `-m "not integration and not external"` set, 5,701 tests)' to match the Context's established terminology.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a criterion (or extend SC5/AC3) to specify whether the `.test_durations` file is committed/stored and how its presence is verified, or explicitly mark it out-of-scope for this epic if it is purely optional infrastructure.
findings.finding: The Scope IN describes 'optional `.test_durations` store' as a deliverable, but no Success Criterion or Acceptance Criterion verifies its presence, format, or behavior — it is uncovered work.
findings.location: Scope IN ('`--durations` per-test logging + optional `.test_durations` store') vs Success Criteria SC5 ('Every gate run logs its slowest tests (`--durations`)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Acceptance Criterion verifying that `test.yml` receives the same xdist, pip-cache, and `--durations` changes as `gerrit-verify.yaml`, or confirm that one of the three child stories explicitly covers `test.yml`.
findings.finding: The Scope IN states changes are 'Applied to `gerrit-verify.yaml` (and `test.yml` for parity)', making `test.yml` a named deliverable, but no Success Criterion or Acceptance Criterion explicitly verifies that `test.yml` was updated — the ACs only reference landing on `main` and child stories, which may not explicitly cover `test.yml`.
findings.location: Scope IN ('Applied to `gerrit-verify.yaml` (and `test.yml` for parity)') vs Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a named Gerrit revert strategy or a temporary `PYTEST_XDIST_DISABLE` env-var escape hatch in the workflow) so the team can quickly restore serial execution if xdist causes CI flakiness or coverage regression post-landing.
findings.finding: The plan introduces xdist parallelization as a CI gate change but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan if the parallel run causes flakiness or coverage regression in production CI. Local experiment evidence is cited, but CI environments may differ; the remedy per the compat criterion requires a stated reversibility mechanism, not just justification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section 'Rollback plan' stating: 'If xdist causes flakiness or coverage regression after landing, revert the `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `test.yml` commits via Gerrit (standard revert flow). As an emergency escape hatch, set `PYTEST_WORKERS=1` (or equivalent env var) in the workflow to restore serial execution without a code revert.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a developer-facing note (in `docs/` or the contributing guide) documenting the xdist test-authoring constraints introduced by this change: no ordering assumptions, session-scoped fixtures must be xdist-safe (`scope='session'` with `--dist worksteal` caveats), and no shared mutable file-system state between workers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces xdist parallelization, which places new implicit constraints on test authors (no reliance on test ordering, session-scoped fixtures must be xdist-safe, no shared mutable state between workers), but no developer-facing documentation or ADR covering these test-authoring constraints is planned beyond the coverage-specific `docs/coverage.md` update.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'docs/coverage.md (and any CI docs) updated'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the `.test_durations` artifact: specify its file format, storage location (repo-committed vs CI artifact vs ephemeral), retention/cleanup policy, and a brief usage note on how to interpret it for identifying slow tests.
findings.finding: The plan mentions an optional `.test_durations` store as a new CI artifact but provides no documentation on its format, location, retention policy, or how developers should interpret/use it for optimization — leaving the artifact undocumented.
findings.location: Scope section — '--durations per-test logging + optional .test_durations store'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan whether the final CI YAML uses `-n auto` (self-configuring, recommended) or a hardcoded `-n<count>`; if hardcoded, document it as a configurable variable (e.g., a YAML `env:` entry or matrix param) so it can be updated when runner specs change.
findings.finding: The worker count passed to pytest-xdist (`-n4` in measured evidence vs. 'pinned to the runner's real core count' in the Success Criteria) is unresolved in the plan: if the CI YAML will literally use `-n4`, this is a hardcoded threshold that will silently produce suboptimal parallelism if the runner's core count changes (GitHub Actions runners are 2-core by default; a future runner upgrade or change of runner type would require a manual YAML edit). The plan never specifies whether `-n auto`, `-n4`, or some computed value will appear in the final YAML.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'pinned to the runner's real core count'; Context — 'pytest -n4 --dist worksteal'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `pytest -n auto --dist worksteal` in the CI YAML so xdist auto-detects the runner's available cores; document the choice in a comment in the YAML and reference it from the updated docs/coverage.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete, numeric install-time target to the pip-cache Success Criterion (e.g., 'cold install ≤ 12s on repeat runs with cache hit, down from ~19s cold') to make the criterion end-state-observable.
findings.finding: The pip-cache Success Criterion lacks a concrete, end-state-observable validation target. The context cites '19s → 9s locally' but the SC only says 'pip cache hit reduces per-job install time on repeat runs' — no numeric floor or before/after target is carried into the testable criterion, making it difficult to verify the goal was actually achieved in CI vs. locally.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'pip cache hit reduces per-job install time on repeat runs' with 'pip cache hit reduces measured install time from ~19s (cold) to ≤ 12s (warm) on repeat CI runs; verified by comparing job logs before and after the cache PR lands.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] (Advisory) Consider documenting a preferred landing sequence (e.g., xdist first as the highest-risk change, then pip-cache, then --durations) so that CI regressions can be bisected cleanly, even though all three are independently releasable.
findings.finding: All three children are currently open in parallel and all touch the same CI workflow files (gerrit-verify.yaml, test.yml); the plan does not articulate a landing sequence. xdist (#1) is the highest-risk change (test-isolation regressions, coverage-combine subtleties) and could mask or interact with the pip-cache and --durations changes if all land simultaneously, making bisection harder if a regression appears post-merge.
findings.location: Scope section / child ticket list (all three shown as open)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'pinned to the runner's real core count' SC with the static -n4/-n3 prescription: either (a) change the SC to say 'pinned to N workers matching the runner's vCPU count' and document WHY static > dynamic (predictability, avoids SQLite race headroom), or (b) specify using `$(nproc)` with an explicit upper bound (e.g., `-n $(( $(nproc) < 6 ? $(nproc) : 4 ))`) to stay safe at any runner size.
findings.finding: The plan states 'pinned to the runner's real core count' in the Success Criteria but the experimental note immediately pins static values (-n4 ubuntu / -n3 macOS arm). The two framings are inconsistent: 'real core count' implies dynamic detection (e.g., `$(nproc)` / `$(sysctl -n hw.ncpu)`), while the text actually prescribes a hardcoded value. If the implementer reads the SC literally and uses `-n auto`, they will contradict the experimentally-proven advice in the Context (SQLite race at high concurrency). If they read the experimental note and hardcode -n4/-n3, the SC claim of 'real core count' becomes inaccurate when GitHub updates runner specs. The plan needs to commit to one mechanism and state its positive rationale.
findings.location: Success Criteria (first bullet) vs. Context / ticket comment: 'Use -n <real-cores>, NOT -n auto. Pin -n4 (ubuntu 4-vCPU) / -n3 (macos arm)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the SC, replace 'pinned to the runner's real core count' with 'pinned to a static worker count matching the known runner vCPU count (-n4 ubuntu-latest, -n3 macos-latest arm), NOT -n auto, because -n auto at high core counts triggers a coverage.py SQLite-combine race (proven at n=18).' Add this rationale to the plan body so implementers don't revert to -n auto.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the role of the optional `.test_durations` store: if `--dist worksteal` is the chosen scheduler, document that `--store-durations` output is not consumed by worksteal and either drop it from scope, or explicitly call out that it is groundwork for a future switch to duration-based scheduling (`--dist loadschedule`).
findings.finding: The plan mentions an 'optional .test_durations store' as a deliverable of the --durations logging story, but .test_durations is the output file of pytest-xdist's --store-durations flag, which is specifically useful for duration-based scheduling (--dist loadschedule / loadfile), NOT for the worksteal scheduler the plan's own experiments validate and recommend. Using --store-durations with --dist worksteal generates the file but xdist ignores it for scheduling decisions, making this an unverified capability assertion about what the feature delivers.
findings.location: Scope section: '--durations per-test logging + optional .test_durations store'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the conflict between '-p no:cacheprovider' (present in both CI files) and the 'optional .test_durations store' deliverable: either explicitly drop -p no:cacheprovider from the xdist-enabled step, scope it to non-xdist runs, or clarify in the plan that .test_durations persistence is local-only and not a CI-run artifact.
findings.finding: The plan asserts an 'optional .test_durations store' as a Scope deliverable, but the existing CI pytest command in both gerrit-verify.yaml and test.yml uses '-p no:cacheprovider', which disables the pytest cache plugin that xdist uses to write .test_durations. Unless the plan explicitly removes or conditionally suppresses -p no:cacheprovider, the .test_durations file will never be written — the stated capability is contradicted by the current CI flag.
findings.location: Scope section ('--durations per-test logging + optional .test_durations store'); gerrit-verify.yaml line 351; test.yml line 370
findings.suggested_fix: In the child story that adds .test_durations, remove '-p no:cacheprovider' from the parallelized pytest step (the original rationale was preventing stale cache leaking across runs on ephemeral runners — xdist's per-run tmpdir isolation makes this safe to drop for the duration-store step), or add an explicit 'pytest --store-durations' invocation as a separate, cacheprovider-enabled step.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
